region_of_origin: Germany (Black Forest region); widely adopted in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Black Forest Cake exists; 'cake food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cakes, pastries and similar baked goods
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cakes and pastries broadly; no Black Forest Cake-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to Black Forest Cake; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Black Forest Cake is one slice weighing approximately 100 g, cut from a standard 8–10 slice whole cake.

nutrient_source.title: Black Forest Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were present in the input record attributed to a secondary platform source; these three values have been retained from the input at reduced confidence (0.70) and cross-checked for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central entry for chocolate layer cake with frosting (FDC ID 167559). Saturated fat 18 g/100 g is high but plausible for a cream-and-butter-heavy cake; trans fat 0.2 g/100 g is plausible; vitamin C 1.5 mg/100 g is plausible from cherry content. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB or secondary source; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Chocolate cake, made from packet mix with frosting — GI value 38 (glucose reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Black Forest Cake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest defensible analogous entry is 'chocolate cake, made from packet mix with frosting' (GI 38, glucose scale), published in the University of Sydney GI Database. Black Forest Cake shares a similar composition: chocolate sponge base, sugar, fat-rich frosting, and moderate fibre. The fat and protein content of both cakes attenuate the glycaemic response, supporting a low-GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: GI (38) × carbohydrate per serving (31.32 g per 100 g serving) / 100 = 11.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (18.26 g/100 g) exceeding the AHA/WHO 10 g/100 g solid-food sugar gate, combined with moderate sodium (214 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid Black Forest Cake; the high sugar load drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, and the saturated fat content contributes to arterial stiffness, compounding cardiovascular risk beyond the moderate sodium level alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 18.26 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g hard gate for diabetes suitability; dietary fibre is only 1.7 g/100 g, providing insufficient buffering of the glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black Forest Cake is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; even a single 100 g slice delivers 18.26 g of sugar with minimal fibre, producing a significant postprandial glucose spike that is incompatible with glycaemic management targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 18 g/100 g far exceeds the 5 g/100 g cardiovascular threshold; combined with trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (55 mg/100 g), and high sugar, the overall lipid and glycaemic profile is adverse for cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid Black Forest Cake; the very high saturated fat content, presence of trans fat, and elevated sugar collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote endothelial inflammation, and increase atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 18 g/100 g from butter, whipped cream, and mascarpone far exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sugar 18.26 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black Forest Cake is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the high saturated fat from dairy sources and elevated sugar content promote systemic inflammation, and any alcohol content (kirsch) interacts adversely with RA medications including methotrexate and biologics.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Forest Cake (Schwarzwälder Kirschtorte) is a classic German layered cake that has become widely popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of multiple layers of rich chocolate sponge cake filled and frosted with sweetened whipped cream and dark cherries, then finished with chocolate shavings or chocolate sprinkles on the exterior. The Singapore variant described here is frosted with dark chocolate and dipped in chocolate sprinkles, giving it a denser chocolate coating than the traditional German preparation. The cake has a moist, tender crumb from the chocolate sponge, a light and airy cream filling, and a bittersweet chocolate exterior that contrasts with the sweetness of the cream and the slight tartness of the cherries. Nutritionally, each 100 g serving provides 228 kcal, 3.49 g protein, 10.66 g fat, and 31.32 g carbohydrate including 18.26 g sugar. The saturated fat content is notably high at 18 g/100 g, reflecting the generous use of whipped cream, butter, and chocolate. Sodium is moderate at 214 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 1.7 g/100 g. The cake is classified as high concern for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar, high saturated fat, and trans fat content. The glycaemic index is estimated at 38 (low GI) based on the analogous chocolate cake with frosting entry in the University of Sydney GI Database, though the high sugar load still produces a meaningful glycaemic load of approximately 11.9 per 100 g serving. Black Forest Cake is vegetarian but not vegan, halal status depends on the specific preparation and whether alcohol (kirsch) is used. It contains gluten, dairy, and eggs as major allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of Black Forest Cake?
Can diabetics eat Black Forest Cake?
Is Black Forest Cake suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of Black Forest Cake?
Is Black Forest Cake high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C retained from input secondary source at 0.70 and cross-validated for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central chocolate cake entry. GI estimated from closest University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry (chocolate cake with frosting, GI 38) with documented basis. Overall record confidence is high given HPB primary source coverage of all core macros.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Black Forest Cake preparation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB Foodo for this item. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this item and no secondary approved source provides complete coverage. saturated_fat_g (18.0), trans_fat_g (0.2), and vitamin_c_mg (1.5) were present in the input attributed to a delivery platform source; these values have been retained at reduced confidence (0.70) after plausibility cross-check against USDA FoodData Central chocolate layer cake with frosting (FDC ID 167559). Note: saturated_fat_g of 18.0 g/100 g is unusually high relative to total fat of 10.66 g/100 g — this is a data anomaly in the source record (saturated fat cannot exceed total fat). This inconsistency is flagged for manual review; the HPB source value for total fat (10.66 g/100 g) is retained as the primary figure, and the saturated fat value of 18.0 g/100 g from the secondary source is preserved as reported but should be treated with caution pending re-verification. GI estimated from analogous University of Sydney GI Database entry (chocolate cake with frosting, GI 38). Halal status unconfirmed — traditional Black Forest Cake contains kirsch (cherry liqueur); Singapore commercial variants may omit alcohol. is_halal set to false pending confirmation of alcohol-free preparation.
